behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on the real LLM's semantic understanding of legal documents. Mocking the extractor would hide failures where the model misses clause types, misclassifies them, or fails on context-dependent extraction. The core failure mode—'extractor fails to identify a key term type that should be present'—cannot be caught without running the actual model on real contracts with diverse structures and phrasing.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: Run the real clause extractor on a corpus of contracts (simple, complex, edge-case formats) with ground-truth clause annotations. Verify extraction accuracy for each clause type (parties, dates, obligations, penalties). Test on contracts with unusual formatting, implicit terms, and edge cases. Measure consistency across multiple runs if using a non-deterministic model. Verify no required clause type is systematically missed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: At Agentic level, we run the real model. Self-check: Could a Unit or Integration mock hide real failures? Yes—mocking would always return predefined clauses, hiding cases where the real model fails to identify a clause type present in the document.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: Detecting euphemistic or indirect problematic language requires the real LLM's nuanced semantic reasoning. Mocking would hide failures where the model misses context-dependent risk signals, misinterprets policy intent, or doesn't recognize indirect phrasing as risky. The core failure mode—'real problematic language goes unflagged'—depends on LLM capability and cannot be caught with predetermined mock outputs.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: Run the real risk assessor on contracts with known indirect/euphemistic problematic language (e.g., vague penalty clauses, subtle liability limits, indirect non-competes, buried cost escalations). Test across multiple risk policy configurations. Verify that indirect phrasing is correctly flagged according to each policy. Test edge cases: ambiguous language, context-dependent risk, policy conflicts. Measure accuracy and false-negative rate.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking would return predefined risk flags regardless of actual phrasing. At Agentic level, we test the model's real ability to recognize nuanced language.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance verification depends on the real LLM's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific requirements and ability to reason about contract clauses against those requirements. Mocking would hide failures in regulatory interpretation, jurisdictional knowledge, or mapping contract terms to applicable regulations. The core failure mode—'compliance violation is not detected'—depends on LLM capability.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: Run the real compliance checker on contracts with known compliance gaps and passing contracts across multiple jurisdictions. Include contracts missing required clauses, non-compliant payment terms, missing regulatory notices. Verify correct identification of violations and correct passes. Test at least 3 distinct jurisdictions with different regulatory frameworks. Measure false-positive and false-negative rates.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real jurisdictional reasoning is required. Mocks would hide the LLM's understanding of which regulations apply and how to verify compliance.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: The merge logic is deterministic—given fixed conflicting inputs, it should always produce the same output with no data loss. No LLM non-determinism is involved. Mocking the three reviewers' outputs is sufficient to fully exercise the merge function's conflict resolution. The core failure mode—'one assessment is dropped or attribution is lost'—is deterministic and can be caught by directly testing merge() with mock inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Mock outputs from three reviewers with known conflicts on the same clause (e.g., extractor→'standard', risk assessor→'high risk'). Call merge() with these inputs. Verify: both conflicting assessments are preserved in output, each is attributed to its source reviewer, output schema is valid, no data is lost, merge is idempotent. Test with multiple conflict scenarios (2-way and 3-way conflicts, different field types).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Merge logic is deterministic—Unit-level tests with mock outputs fully exercise the behavior. No LLM involvement needed. Self-check: Could a lower level catch this? No, we need to call the merge function. Could a higher level catch something Unit misses? No—there's no non-determinism or external dependency.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: Testing the orchestration (dispatch, parallel collection, merge without loss) requires multiple modules wired together. Unit tests of individual merge logic (Behavior 4) don't exercise the dispatch/collection/coordination flow. Integration level with mocked reviewers catches data loss, race conditions, and orchestration failures. The core failure mode—'a reviewer's output is lost in collection or dispatch fails'—is deterministic in the orchestration layer and doesn't require real reviewers, though real reviewers (Workflow) would provide stronger end-to-end verification.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Mock all three reviewers to return realistic, diverse outputs (varying numbers of findings, different clause types). Run the full orchestration pipeline on realistic contracts. Verify: all three reviewers are dispatched, all outputs collected without loss, results merged with no findings dropped, parallel dispatch completes without race conditions, unified report contains all findings with correct attribution. Test with contracts of varying complexity. Verify output schema validity.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: At Integration level, we test orchestration—dispatch, collection, merge—with mocked reviewers on realistic data. Self-check: Could a Unit test catch 'lost findings in collection'? No, needs full orchestration flow. Could a higher level (Workflow with real reviewers) catch something Integration misses? Yes—unexpected reviewer failures or schema mismatches in real outputs—but Integration is sufficient for the stated behavior.
